sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient presents with right shoulder pain that has been ongoing for several weeks, initially developing after a few days of strenuous work in his backyard.
The pain is located on the outside of the shoulder and is exacerbated by movement, but not present at rest.
The patient has a history of similar pain in the same shoulder from a past injury, but this episode is more localized to the outside of the shoulder.
The patient reports no numbness or tingling in the arm or hands.
The patient has been managing the pain with Advil and icing, which provides some relief.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of injuring the right shoulder playing baseball in the past, but this episode is distinct from that injury.
Review of systems: No additional symptoms in other organs or systems mentioned.
Current medications: I prescribed the patient to continue taking Tylenol as needed for pain management.
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's vital signs are as follows: temperature 97.8°F, heart rate 62 beats per minute, respirations 16 breaths per minute, blood pressure 116/84 mmHg, oxygen saturation 99%.
Physical exam: The patient has limited active and passive range of motion of the right shoulder, with tenderness at the greater tuberosity of the humerus.
The patient has a good hand grip, and on neurovascular exam of the right arm, capillary refill is less than 3 seconds and sensation to touch is intact.
Test Results: The patient's shoulder X-ray shows no fracture.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient's symptoms are most likely due to rotator cuff tendinopathy, specifically injury to the tendons of the muscles that help make the shoulder muscles.
Problem list: Rotator cuff tendinopathy; Suspected injury to the tendons of the muscles that help make the shoulder muscles.
Plan:
I referred the patient to physical therapy for approximately 6-8 weeks to address the rotator cuff tendinopathy.
I also recommended continuing to take Tylenol as needed for pain management.
If the patient's symptoms do not improve, we can consider a steroid injection for the shoulder.
Follow-up: The patient will follow up with me in 6-8 weeks to assess progress with physical therapy and adjust the treatment plan as needed.
